step2_rag_chunks: Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

step2_rag_chunks: Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.

step2_rag_chunks: Smoking is often tied to a habit loop â€” a cue triggers the urge, smoking is the routine, and relief is the reward. Understanding what your specific cues are is the first step. It could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or even a person. Once we identify the cue, we can work on changing just the routine while keeping the relief.
Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.
Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.

step2_rag_chunks: A craving is not a command â€” it is a wave. It builds, peaks, and fades, usually within 3 to 5 minutes. Urge surfing means observing the craving without acting on it â€” noticing where you feel it in your body, watching it get stronger, and then watching it pass. Each time you surf an urge without smoking, it gets slightly easier the next time.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Withdrawal symptoms usually peak within the first few days after quitting and gradually decrease over time.

step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.

step2_rag_chunks: After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

step2_rag_chunks: Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.

Smoking is often tied to a habit loop â€” a cue triggers the urge, smoking is the routine, and relief is the reward. Understanding what your specific cues are is the first step. It could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or even a person. Once we identify the cue, we can work on changing just the routine while keeping the relief.
Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.
